coredata.dc:title: Gold nanoparticles-mediated ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor for ultra-sensitive detection of Abscisic Acid
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yongqiang
Lin, Lei
Wei, Yuzhen
Li, Wenting
Nie, Pengcheng
He, Yong
Feng, Xuping
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel ratiometric aptasensor based on carbon quantum dots@2-Methylimidazole zinc salt (CQDs@ZIF-8) and aptamer-functionalized gold nanoparticles (Apt-AuNPs) was developed for highly sensitive detection of ABA by fluorescence spectrometry. The CQDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterials displayed dual-emission properties at 490 nm and 657 nm with excitation at 420 nm were synthesized for the first time. ZIF-8 not only served as an anchor point for CQDs but also acted as a modulator to regulate fluorescence signals of CQDs. Interestingly, introduction of ZIF-8 changed the quenching properties of the AuNPs on CQDs. The AuNPs quenched the fluorescence of CQDs@ZIF-8 at 490 nm but not at the second peak of 657 nm. Few studies have been reported on the ineffectiveness of AuNPs in fluorescence quenching as far as we know. In this study, we found that incorporation of ABA triggered the aggregation of AuNPs due to the specific ABA-aptamer recognition and this changed the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric probe (CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs). The proposed probe increased the sensitivity and selectivity of determining ABA levels in rice seeds in the range of 0.100–150 ng/mL with an LOD of 30.0 ng/L. Importantly, the method proposed here offers a new unique strategy for the construction of ratiometric probes and ultra-sensitive measurement of biomolecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
ZIF-8
Ratiometric probe
Abscisic acid
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs with dual-emission properties
Use of the probe to determine the concentration of ABA in rice seeds
Characterization of morphology and chemical structures
Detection of ABA through ratiometric fluorescence
Selectivity and stability of the aptasensor
Testing the ability of the probe to detect ABA in rice seeds

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32071895 ) and the Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province ( 2019B020216001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the preparation of (a1) CQDs and (b1) CQDs@ZIF-8 and the corresponding TEM images for (a2) CQDs and (b2) CQDs@ZIF-8; SEM images of the prepared (c) CQDs@ZIF-8 and (d) CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs; (e) Energy distribution of elemental Au on the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanostructures.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of the prepared nanomaterials; (c) The full XPS spectrum of CQDs@ZIF-8; The high resolution spectrum of (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s and (f) Zn 2p.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the prepared materials; (b) The wavelength dependence of the photoluminescence intensities of the synthetic CQDs and (c) the corresponding contour map; (d) The fluorescence spectra of the prepared materials; The effects of Apt-AuNPs on the fluorescence properties of (e) CQDs and (f) CQDs@ZIF-8.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs before and after adding of 5.0 ng/mL of ABA; (b) SEM images of CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs after the addition of ABA; (c) The electron energy spectrum of element Au obtained by scanning along the ray direction from Fig. 4 (b); (d) TEM images of Apt-AuNPs before (inset) and after the addition of ABA.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs probe exposed to different concentrations of ABA (0–150 ng/mL); (b) The dependence of the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 657 nm and 490 nm of the ratiometric probe on ABA concentration; (c) Response of the ratiometric probe to several potential interferences with 10 ng/mL of ABA and 100 ng/mL of other interfering compounds; (d) Selectivity of the ratiometric probes in detecting ABA (10 ng/mL) and interfering substances at same concentration as ABA.
A schematic diagram of the ratiometric probe for the detection of ABA in rice seeds.
Concentration of ABA in rice seeds, determined through the fluorescence and HPLC-MS methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Paired t-test: p < 0.0500, significant difference; p > 0.0500, no significant difference.

head.ce:title: Gold nanoparticles-mediated ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor for ultra-sensitive detection of Abscisic Acid

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huanan Industrial Technology Research Institute of Zhejiang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel ratiometric aptasensor based on carbon quantum dots@2-Methylimidazole zinc salt (CQDs@ZIF-8) and aptamer-functionalized gold nanoparticles (Apt-AuNPs) was developed for highly sensitive detection of ABA by fluorescence spectrometry. The CQDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterials displayed dual-emission properties at 490 nm and 657 nm with excitation at 420 nm were synthesized for the first time. ZIF-8 not only served as an anchor point for CQDs but also acted as a modulator to regulate fluorescence signals of CQDs. Interestingly, introduction of ZIF-8 changed the quenching properties of the AuNPs on CQDs. The AuNPs quenched the fluorescence of CQDs@ZIF-8 at 490 nm but not at the second peak of 657 nm. Few studies have been reported on the ineffectiveness of AuNPs in fluorescence quenching as far as we know. In this study, we found that incorporation of ABA triggered the aggregation of AuNPs due to the specific ABA-aptamer recognition and this changed the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric probe (CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs). The proposed probe increased the sensitivity and selectivity of determining ABA levels in rice seeds in the range of 0.100–150 ng/mL with an LOD of 30.0 ng/L. Importantly, the method proposed here offers a new unique strategy for the construction of ratiometric probes and ultra-sensitive measurement of biomolecules.

ce:section.ce:para: Abscisic Acid (ABA) is an essential phytohormone involved in regulating plant growth (including seed germination and seedling development) and responses to abiotic stress (Nguyen et al., 2021). Quantitative detection of ABA is therefore important in assessing the physiological status of plants. Notably, conventional analytical techniques such as High-performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) (Bai et al., 2018), Gas or Liquid Chromatography/Mass Spectrometry (GC or LC/MS) (Zou et al., 2019; Yalçın et al., 2019), Capillary Electrophoresis (CE) (Chen et al., 2018) and Enzyme-linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA) (Yi et al., 2020), have all been used successfully to detect and assay plant hormones. However, these methods are not suitable for research on rapid hormone signal transduction responses because they involve the complicated sample pretreatment step or require expensive specialized instruments and skilled technicians (Shi et al., 2020). To circumvent these problems, nanomaterial-based biosensing techniques have been developed to detect biomolecules (Liu et al., 2019; Khanmohammadi et al., 2020).

Currently, fluorescence spectrometry is extensively used because of its simplicity, high sensitivity, good selectivity and low cost (Tang et al., 2020). In addition, several reports have highlighted the use of fluorescence spectrometry in the detection of plant biomolecules (Sharmah et al., 2019; Asefpour Vakilian, 2020; Meng et al., 2021). For example, Huang et al. (2019) used a red fluorescence probe (NBD-O-1) to detect Cysteine and Glutathion in Arabidopsis thaliana with Limits of Detection (LOD) of 0.061 μM and 0.082 μM, respectively. Additionally, Zeng et al. (2021) developed a water soluble quinolin-containing nitrone derivative T for the fluorometric sensing of hypochlorite in A. thaliana, within the range of 1.0 × 10−7 to 2.0 × 10−4 M. Nevertheless, these methods involve complex steps of organic synthesis and use single-emissive intensity as an output signal, which is susceptible to the fluctuation of excitation source and environmental interference. As a result, ratiometric probes with dual-emission properties are currently being designed to detect biomolecules, by determining the ratio of fluorescence intensity from dual-fluorophores as the final index signal. Notably, such methods are resistant to environmental interference (Zhao et al., 2015; Li et al., 2020; Qian et al., 2020).

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are emerging photoluminescent nanomaterials that have drawn considerable attention for their unique fluorescent properties, cost-effective synthesis and good biocompatibility (Wang et al., 2019a). However, although they have been used to detect various biomolecules (Walther et al., 2020; Mahmoud et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020), most of them only have single-emission properties, which reduces the reliability of analysis. Therefore, developing CQDs with dual-emission properties instead of using two fluorescent molecules to construct ratiometric fluorescent probes, might be a better strategy for the detection of target molecules. Another limitation is that synthesized CQDs are usually prone to aggregation, leading to the self-quenching of fluorescence during storage, which in turn affects their sensing performance (Wang et al., 2020a). It is therefore necessary to treat prepared CQDs to prevent them from aggregating. Moreover, the 2-Methylimidazole zinc salt (ZIF-8) with its zeolite-type imidazolium salt backbone is a type of Metal-organic Framework (MOF) with great chemical stability and excellent light absorption (Cao et al., 2019). Because of its highly porous structure, ZIF-8 has a large internal surface areas and uniform cavities, into which introduced objects can be distributed and their aggregation avoided (Wu et al., 2020). Therefore, a fluorescent probe created by loading ZIF-8 with CQDs might have better long-term storage stability. On the other hand, aptamers are nucleic-acid, antibody analogs with high affinity and specificity for their target molecules (Fazlali et al., 2021). Recently, Grozio et al. (2013) successfully selected aptamers for ABA using the systematic evolution of ligands through the exponential enrichment (SELEX) procedure, the aptamers showed high binding affinity to ABA with dissociation constants values of 8.0 × 10−7 M. This may offer an opportunity for the specific quantitative detection of ABA.

Given this background, the present study developed a novel ratiometric aptasensor based on CQDs@ZIF-8 and aptamer-functionalized gold nanoparticles (Apt-AuNPs) for high-sensitive detection of ABA. Herein, the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterial was designed to have dual-emission properties (Scheme 1 ). Notably, the ZIF-8 in the nanomaterial not only served as an anchor point for CQDs but also acted as a signal modulator to regulate the intensity of fluorescence and peak positions of the CQDs. The detailed mechanism by which the probe operates and its practical use to detect ABA in rice seeds are here presented and discussed.

Glutathione (GSH), formamide, Zinc nitrate hexahydrate and 2-methylimidazole (MelM) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai China). In addition, ABA was obtained from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) while the aptamers were provided by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai China), using the following nucleic acid sequence (Grozio et al., 2013): 5′–SH–GCG GAT GAA GAC TGG TGT GAG GGG ATG GGT TAG GTG GAG GTGGTT ATT CCG GGA ATT CGC CCT AAA TAC GAG CAA C-3'. The other reagents (e.g., HAuCl4·4H2O, methanol and sodium citrate) were obtained from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The rice seeds were provided by the Institute of Agricultural Information Technology of Zhejiang University. All the experiments were performed using ultrapure water (Millipore, ≥18 MΩ cm−1).

The morphology of the prepared nanomaterials was examined using a JEM-1230 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) and the SU-8010 Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) was conducted using an Oxford Aztec X-Max 80 energy spectrometer (Oxford Instruments, Oxford, UK). The particle size and zeta potential were also determined using the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Analyzer (Malvern, Malvern, UK) while the X-ray Diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained using the D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany). Additionally, the FT-IR spectra were obtained using a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (ThermoFisher, Massachusetts, USA). Moreover, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were taken using the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (ThermoFisher, Massachusetts, USA) and fluorescent spectra were obtained using the F97XP fluorescence spectrometer (Lengguang technology, Shanghai, China). Furthermore, the UV–vis spectra were obtained using the Shimadzu UV-1601 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) while fluorescence lifetime measurement was carried out using the Quantaurus-Tau fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Hamamatsu Photonics, Hamamatsu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs with dual-emission properties
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs with the dual-emission property were prepared through a hydrothermal method (Shao et al., 2019). Briefly, 0.34 g of GSH was mixed with 20 mL of formamide and the mixture was stirred for 10 min followed by ultrasonication for 10 min. Thereafter, the mixed solution was transferred into a reaction kettle for hydrothermal reactions which lasted for 24 h at 200 °C, to obtain the raw CQD products. The raw products were then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min, after which the supernatant was collected and dialyzed using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 48 h, to obtain purified CQDs. Finally, the purified CQDs were freeze-dried and preserved at 4 °C for further use.

CQDs@ZIF-8 was prepared through the facile one-step solvothermal route (Chang et al., 2020). Briefly, 300 mg of 2-methylimidazole was dissolved in 16 mL of methanol, then 2 mL of the purified CQDs was added dropwise into the solution while stirring, to form a precursor solution. Thereafter, 120 mg of zinc nitrate hexahydrate was added into the precursor solution, followed by magnetic stirring for 2 h at 70 °C. Afterwards, the reaction product was washed thrice with ethanol and distilled water respectively to obtain the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterials, which were then centrifuged at 12000 rpm at 4 °C for 15 min. Finally, the purified product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

AuNPs were synthesized by reducing gold chloride with trisodium citrate (Balasubramanian et al., 2010). Briefly, 2 mL of sodium citrate (38.8 mM) was rapidly injected into a boiling aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (20 mL, 1.0 mM) with fierce stirring for 30 min. Synthesis of AuNPs was confirmed when the solution turned from pale yellow to wine red. Thereafter, the obtained solution was cooled to room temperature then stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C. In order to synthesize Apt-AuNPs, the ABA aptamer was annealed at 95 °C for 5 min, prior to use. In addition, 500 μL of AuNPs was incubated with the ABA aptamer (150 μL, 1 μM) overnight at room temperature, to form Apt-AuNPs.

350 μL of the CQDs@ZIF-8 solution (0.2 mg/mL) was first incubated with 100 μL of Apt-AuNPs for 30 min. Thereafter, 50 μL of ABA standard solutions at different concentrations were added into the above mixture. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded and the corresponding standard curve was established based on the intensity of the measured fluorescence from the two emission peaks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of the probe to determine the concentration of ABA in rice seeds
ce:section.ce:para: Given that ABA plays an important role in regulating the germination of rice seeds, rice seeds were used to evaluate the effectiveness of the probe in detecting the levels of ABA. The sample seeds were pretreated as previously described but with some modifications (Xiao et al., 2018). Briefly, the peeled rice seeds were sterilized with ethanol (75%) for 3 min then washed three times in sterilized water. Thereafter, they were put in distilled water and incubated at 28 °C with sufficient light to germinate for 0 h, 24 h or 36 h. The seeds were then transferred into an Eppendorf tube, frozen in liquid nitrogen then milled using a commercial grinder. Afterwards, 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added into the Eppendorf tube which was then kept at 4 °C overnight. The upper layer of liquid was then collected, washed with 100 μL of an aqueous solution of HCl (0.2%) and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and the final volume was adjusted to 1 mL with methanol. Finally, the level of ABA was measured as described in section 2.2.4 and also through the standard High-performance Liquid Chromatography-mass Spectrometry (HPLC-MS) method (Wang et al., 2020b).

All the molecular calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 program. The molecular structures of CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 were optimized using the Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the Becke's three parameter (B3) exchange functional and the Lee-Yang-Parr (LYP) correlation function for C, H, N and O atoms and the LANL2DZ basis set for Zn metal (Falahati et al., 2018). The optimized structures, infrared spectrum, distribution of electrostatic potentials and frontier orbitals were visualized using Gauss View 5.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of morphology and chemical structures
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, CQDs with cyan and red emission were synthesized for the first time using GSH and formamid as precursors (Fig. 1 (a1) ) and CQDs@ZIF-8 was prepared to prevent aggregation (Fig. 1 (b1)). The TEM images of CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 are shown in Fig. 1 (a2) and (b2). The CQDs were nearly-spherical and remained relatively dispersed with a mean particle diameter of 3.39 nm (Fig. S1 (a)) and mean zeta potential of −21.4 mV (Fig. S2 (a)). Additionally, TEM revealed that the CQDs@ZIF-8 appeared hexagonal with a fuzzy appearance, indicating that they were stereoscopic. The SEM results (Fig. 1 (c)) also showed that the CQDs@ZIF-8 had a polyhedron shape, consistent with the configuration of typical ZIF-8 as previously reported (Li et al., 2019). Notably, most of the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanostructures had rough morphologies with irregularly shaped and unclear borders, which can be attributed to the doping of CQDs. The average diameter of CQDs@ZIF-8 was 194 nm (Fig. S1 (b)).

Moreover, the SEM images of the mixture of Apt-AuNPs and CQDs@ZIF-8 (Apt-AuNPs@CQDs@ZIF-8) revealed binding between the two (Fig. 1 (d)) and this was confirmed through Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. 1 (e), the distribution of element Au coincided with that of CQDs@ZIF-8, indicating that Apt-AuNPs were linked to the surface of CQDs@ZIF-8, probably because of the electrostatic attraction force between them. As shown in Fig. S2 (b) and (c), CQDs@ZIF-8 and Apt-AuNPs had positive (43.0 mV) and negative (−26.2 mV) charges, respectively, while Apt-AuNPs@CQDs@ZIF-8 had a zeta potential of 27.6 mV (Fig. S2 (d)). These results confirmed the presence of electrostatic interaction between the molecules. The distribution of elements C, N and Zn on the Apt-AuNPs@CQDs@ZIF-8 in Fig. 1 (d) is also shown in Fig. S3. Notably, elements C was fairly evenly distributed but N and Zn were mainly distributed in Apt-AuNPs@CQDs@ZIF-8, which may be related to the chemical composition of CQDs@ZIF-8.

The XRD patterns of the prepared nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 2 (a) . Notably, CQDs@ZIF-8 had the same diffraction peaks as ZIF-8 and these characteristic peak positions matched the crystal structure of ZIF-8 as previously reported (Taheri et al., 2020). Interestingly, CQDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a broad diffraction peak at around 25.8°, which may be attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic (Yang et al., 2019). The results therefore demonstrated the successful preparation of CQDs@ZIF-8. Thereafter, the chemical characterization was conducted through FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2 (b), CQDs displayed functional groups related to glutathione. The absorption peaks located at 1120 and 1675 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O and CO groups. On the other hand, the peaks at 1385 and 3440 cm−1 can be attributed to the C–N and N–H stretching vibrations (Wu et al., 2014). Interestingly, the absorption peak of the S–H group at 2500 cm−1, belonging to glutathione, was not observed in the CQDs, suggesting that the S–H group of glutathione had reacted with the N–H group of formamide to form hydrogen sulfide. Moreover, the peak intensities of the CO and C–O groups in CQDs@ZIF-8 were significantly lower and higher than those of CQDs, respectively, probably due to the breakage of the CO double bond and formation of the coordination bond of the Zn–O group between CQDs and ZIF-8 (Borah and Devi, 2020).

The chemical structure of CQDs@ZIF-8 was further confirmed through X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The full XPS spectrum of CQDs@ZIF-8 is shown in Fig. 2 (c). The strong peaks at 285.9, 399.0, 531.6, 1021.8 and 1045.1 eV belonged to C1s, N1s, O1s, Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively. Fig. 2 (d) reveals the high-resolution spectrum of C1s, where the peaks at 284.2, 284.8, 286.3 and 288.2 eV corresponded to CC, C–C, C–N/C–O and CO/CN bonds, respectively. The presence of CO and C–O groups further confirmed binding between CQDs and ZIF-8. In addition, the N1s spectra (Fig. 2 (e)) had peaks at 397.1, 398.9 and 400.6 eV, which were attributed to the C–N, CN and N–H groups, respectively (Yu et al., 2014). The two signal peaks at 1021.9 eV (Zn–O) and 1044.9 eV (Zn 2p1/2) were also observed in the Zn 2p spectra (Fig. 2 (f)). These results were consistent with those obtained from FT-IR analysis.

The above chemical characterization indicated successful synthesis of CQDs@ZIF-8 through the solvothermal method. As shown in Fig. S4, GSH has three binding sites for formamid, and the hydroxyl groups (O–H) at both ends of GSH can form covalent amide bonds with the amino groups (N–H) of formamid through a condensation reaction (Wang et al., 2013). In addition, the sulfhydryl group (S–H) from GSH reacts with the amino groups to form hydrogen sulfide. This reaction results in the synthesis of CQDs with relatively stable structures after prolonged incubation. The simulated infrared spectrum (Fig. S5) of the proposed CQDs structure had distinct peaks at 3280 cm−1, 1648 cm−1, 1352 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1. These results were consistent with the findings from FT-IR analysis of CQDs (Fig. 2 (b)), highlighting the reliability of the structural model. The CQDs were then used to prepare CQDs@ZIF-8 through the facile hydrothermal method, where Zn2+ ions were first bound to MelM to form the ZIF-8 unit. Thereafter, Zn atoms from ZIF-8 were combined with the amino and carbonyl groups in the CQDs to form a four-coordinated structure. Therefore, the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterial was successfully prepared as designed.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the prepared materials are shown in Fig. 3 (a) . The CQDs had a broad absorption peak at around 400 nm, which can be attributed to the n–π* transition of the CO bonds in CQDs. Additionally, CQDs@ZIF-8 showed a distinct absorption peak at 220 nm, which have been due to the organic ligands of ZIF-8 (Wang et al., 2018). The prepared Apt-AuNPs also had a typical absorption peak at 520 nm, consistent with existing literature (Zhang et al., 2019). Fig. 3 (b) shows the wavelength dependence of the PL intensities of the synthetic CQDs and the corresponding contour map is shown in Fig. 3 (c). As the wavelength changed from 350 nm to 450 nm, the CQDs exhibited dual emission properties. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of one peak decreased with a gradual red-shift while that of the other peak increased gradually and reached a maximum when the excitation wavelength was set at 420 nm. Although the emission of CQDs at 438 nm was high when excited at 360 nm, excitation at 420 nm was chosen as the optimal wavelength in this study because it is safer and harmless to plants.

The fluorescence spectra of the prepared materials are shown in Fig. 3 (d) while the fluorescent pictures are highlighted in Fig. S6. Compared to CQDs, CQDs@ZIF-8 had dual-emission peaks at 490 nm and 657 nm, with a red shift of the emission maxima when excited at 420 nm, which may have been due to the Intramolecular Charge Transfer (ICT) effect (Wang et al., 2019b). In order to understand the change in charge transfer, the electrostatic potentials of the CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 were calculated based on the Density Functional Theory (DFT) as shown in Fig. S7. The results showed that CQDs had an overall negatively charged surface while strong positive charges were present on the surface of CQDs@ZIF-8. This indicated the existence of charge transfer between the CQDs and ZIF-8 units. Moreover, Frontier Molecular Orbital (FMO) analysis was carried out to assess the cause of red shift in the emission spectra. The calculated Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) –Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) energy gaps (ΔE) for CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 were 5.758 eV and 3.393 eV, respectively (Fig. S8). The band gap of CQDs@ZIF-8 was obviously lower than that of CQDs. Based on the FMO theory, the first emission spectra of CQDs were red shifted due to the decrease in the HOMO–LUMO energy gap upon the introduction of the ZIF-8 nanomaterial. Notably, the dual-emission peaks of CQDs@ZIF-8 significantly changed with the addition of Apt-AuNPs (Fig. 3 (d)).

Consequently, the effects of Apt-AuNPs on the fluorescence properties of CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 were investigated. The findings revealed that the fluorescence intensities of both peaks of CQDs were effectively quenched as more Apt-AuNPs (from 0 μL to 180 μL) was added into the CQDs solution (Fig. 3 (e)). Interestingly, there was a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@ZIF-8 at 490 nm while that (fluorescence intensity) at 657 nm increased with the addition of Apt-AuNPs from 0 μL to 100 μL (Fig. 3 (f)). Given that AuNPs have a high molar extinction coefficient (108 cm−1 M−1) with wide absorption spectra, they are known to be excellent quenchers of fluorescence. Additionally, previous reports only showed that AuNPs could quench the fluorescence of CQDs or other fluorophores (Chen and Hildebrandt, 2020). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to show that AuNPs can simultaneously enhance and quench fluorescence.

Generally, the fluorescence quenching mechanism includes Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), Inner Filter Effect (IFE), dynamic quenching and static quenching. The results in Fig. S9 show that the emission spectra of CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 overlapped with the absorption spectrum of Apt-AuNPs, indicating that the above mechanism might exist in their respective systems. Fig. S10 shows the fluorescence lifetime profiles of the CQDs before (a) and after (b) the addition of Apt-AuNPs. Notably, Apt-AuNPs had no significant effect on the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs. This suggested that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Apt-AuNPs was static quenching, indicating the presence of IFE rather than FRET which is a dynamic quenching process. In order for FRET to occur, the distance between the donor (CQDs) and the acceptor (Apt-AuNPs) should be small enough (<10 nm) and there should be a large overlap between the fluorescence spectra of the donor and the absorption spectra of the acceptor (Chen and Hildebrandt, 2020). The results in Fig. S2 (a) and (c) show that both CQDs and Apt-AuNPs had similar negative charges, which prevented them from getting closer. For the CQDs@ZIF-8 system, Apt-AuNPs had opposite charges to those of CQDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. S2 (b)), suggesting that their electrostatic interaction was theoretically possible. Moreover, SEM analysis of CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs (Fig. 1 (d)) confirmed the binding affinities between CQDs@ZIF-8 and Apt-AuNPs. Therefore, it can be concluded that FRET occurred in the CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs system.

The fluorescence spectra of CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs before and after the addition of 5.0 ng/mL of ABA (Fig. 4 (a) ) showed that there was an increase in emission intensity at 490 nm while the peak intensity decreased at 657 nm. This phenomenon may have occurred due to the blocking of FRET in the CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs system. In order to confirm this, SEM was used to examine the microstructure of the materials. The results showed that the addition of ABA to CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs resulted in the clustering of the Apt-AuNPs previously dispersed on the surface of CQDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 4 (b)). This was confirmed by the electron energy spectrum of element Au by scanning along the ray direction from Fig. 4 (b) using EDS (Fig. 4 (c)). Moreover, the effect of ABA on Apt-AuNPs was examined through TEM in order to provide more direct evidence. The findings revealed that addition of ABA resulted in the partial aggregation of Apt-AuNPs, compared to their monodispersed state (inset), as shown in Fig. 4 (d). It is therefore possible that when ABA was added to the CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs system, it combined with the Apt-AuNPs and broke the electrostatic interaction with CQDs@ZIF-8. This in turn made the Apt-AuNPs unstable and led to their aggregation. More importantly, the clustering of Apt-AuNPs increased the distance between Apt-AuNPs and CQDs@ZIF-8, and hence hindering the process of energy transfer between them. As a result, the fluorescence intensities at both peaks changed following the addition of ABA. Based on these results, a schematic representation of the use of the proposed probe for the detection of ABA is shown in Scheme 1.

The study then examined and optimized the factors affecting the performance of the probe. Firstly, the volume of the CQDs was fixed to 2.0 mL, then the effects of the mass ratio of zinc nitrate and MelM on the fluorescence properties of CQDs were investigated. It was observed that as the mass ratio changed from 1.0:0.5 to 1.0:3.5, there was a progressive decrease in the ratio of the fluorescence intensity at 657 nm and 490 nm (I657/I490) although it stabilized at 1.0:2.5 (Fig. S11 (a)). This suggested that MelM plays an important role in charge transfer between CQDs and ZIF-8. A mass ratio of 1.0:2.5 was therefore chosen to prepare the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterials. Additionally, the results in Fig. S11 (b) show that the fluorescence intensity of the probe was sensitive to pH and an optimal pH value of 7.2 was selected for subsequent experiments. Notably, the volume of the aptamer (1.0 μM) used to modify the AuNPs (100 μL) might also affect the detection sensitivity of the probe. This possibility was therefore examined by using a test sample with 10 ng/mL of ABA. The findings showed that the output signal approached its minimum when the volume of the aptamer was (Fig. S11 (c)) and this volume (30 μL) was subsequently used as the standard. Moreover, a reaction incubation time of 30 min was chosen since tests showed that this was sufficient in all the experiments (Fig. S11 (d)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ABA through ratiometric fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Having optimized the conditions, the relationship between the fluorescence signal and the concentration of ABA was then examined. As the concentration of ABA increased from 0 to 150 ng/mL, there was a gradual increase in peak intensity at 490 nm and there was also a decrease in fluorescence emission at 657 nm (Fig. 5 (a) ). This can be attributed to the fact that addition of ABA changed the state of Apt-AuNPs, which cancelled the FRET effect between CQDs@ZIF-8 and Apt-AuNPs, further resulting to fluorescence recovery and reduction at 490 nm and 657 nm, respectively. In addition, the ratios of fluorescence intensity at 657 nm and 490 nm were negatively correlated with the concentrations of ABA (Fig. 5 (b)) and the relation could be estimated using two linear regression equations as follows: Y = −0.025X+1.277 (R2 = 0.991) and Y = −0.002X+1.033 (R2 = 0.995), for the respective ranges of 0.100–10.0 ng/mL and 10.0 − 150 ng/mL. Moreover, the Limit of Detection (LOD) of the assay was calculated to be 30.0 ng/L, based on the principle of triple Signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3). A comparison of the characteristics and analytical performance of the approach developed by the study with other methods is summarized in Table S1. The comparison shows that the performance of the proposed method was comparable or superior to that of the other approaches. These results suggested that the CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs probe may be used for detecting ABA with the advantages of low cost, high sensitivity and rapid response. Nonetheless, the method still has several limitations. For example, the developed probe cannot be recycled or reused, which is what we will try to conquer in our future work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity and stability are important aspects in a probe designed for practical use. In this study, 10.0 ng/mL of ABA and 100 ng/mL of other potential interfering compounds (K+, Na+, Cl−, Ca2+, Mg2+, PO4 3−, Fe2+, Ethylene (EL), Salicylic Acid (SA), Jasmonic Acid (JA), Indoleacetic Acid (IAA) and Gibberellic Acid (GA)) were used to examine the selectivity of the proposed probe. The results showed that the interfering molecules had no significant effect on the signal output of the probe even at a concentration that was 10-fold higher than that of ABA (Fig. 5 (c)). Mixtures of these interfering compounds with the same concentration of ABA (10.0 ng/mL) were also used as controls to verify the results. Addition of ABA resulted in dramatic changes in the fluorescence intensities of the probe (Fig. 5 (d)), which could be attributed to the specific recognition of ABA by the aptamers (Grozio et al., 2013). Besides, the effect of aptamer on the selectivity of the biosensor was also explored. The CQDs@ZIF-8@AuNPs probe without aptamer was prepared and then exposed to 10.0 ng/mL of ABA. As shown in Fig. S12 (a), the response value (I657/I490) of CQDs@ZIF-8@AuNPs probe remained almost unchanged with the addition of ABA. That is, the prepared probe without aptamer had little response to ABA, suggesting the aptamers played a critical role in the specific detection of ABA. These results therefore suggested that the proposed method had good selectivity in detecting ABA. To check the stability of the probe, CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs solutions stored in a closed cartridge in a fridge at 4 °C for 0–30 days were tested. The results were shown in Fig. S12 (b), as the storage time extended, the fluorescence intensities at 490 nm and 657 nm showed slight change trend, and the response value presented a nonlinear dependence on the storage time. A minor decay of 3.8% for the output signal of the probe could be observed after 30 days of storage, indicating that the probe had excellent long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing the ability of the probe to detect ABA in rice seeds
ce:section.ce:para: The ability and usefulness of the protocol developed herein in determining the levels of ABA was tested using rice seeds germinated for 0, 24 or 36 h (Fig. S13). The estimated levels of ABA in the rice seeds ranged from 16.9 to 54.8 ng/g (Table 1 ) while those determined by the standard HPLC-MS method ranged from 17.7 to 55.1 ng/g. In addition, the relative standard deviations (RSD, N = 6) of the fluorescence method were less than 4.57% and the p values (>0.0500) obtained from the paired t-test revealed no significant difference between the two methods. Moreover, the concentration of ABA in the rice seeds decreased rapidly with the incubation time, suggesting that low levels of ABA are more favorable for seed germination (Xiao et al., 2018). These results therefore indicated that the proposed probe was sufficiently robust and reliable for use in practical applications.

The present study developed a simple and highly sensitive fluorescence sensing platform based on a CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs probe for the detection of ABA. Notably, the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterials developed by the study have dual emissions at 490 nm and 657 nm with excitation at 420 nm. Additionally, ZIF-8 not only acts as the anchor point for CQDs but is also a signal modulator that regulates the fluorescence properties of CQDs due to the ICT effect. A detailed theoretical study was also performed using DFT calculations. Moreover, AuNPs (which are excellent fluorescence quenchers) quenched the fluorescence of CQDs@ZIF-8 at 490 nm but enhanced the fluorescence at 657 nm, rather than the quenching fluorescence at both positions, which can be explained by the FRET and IFE theories. Upon the addition of ABA to CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs, ABA combined with Apt-AuNPs because of the specific recognition between ABA and aptamers, resulting to aggregation of AuNPs which changed the fluorescence of the probe. Based on these observations, the ratiometric fluorescent probe was employed to detect ABA, using the ratio of the two fluorescence peaks as the output signal. The results showed that the probe had excellent sensitivity and selectivity for ABA, within the range of 0.100 ng/mL−150 ng/mL with an LOD of 30.0 ng/L. Finally, the probe was used to estimate the concentration of ABA in rice seeds and the results were similar to those obtained from the standard HPLC-MS method. Overall, the method proposed here may provide useful insights for the construction of ratiometric probes and can be utilized in the ultra-sensitive detection of biomolecules.

Yongqiang Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Lei Lin: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yuzhen Wei: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Wenting Li: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Pengcheng Nie: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yong He: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xuping Feng: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32071895) and the Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province (2019B020216001).

ce:section.ce:para:
